title: XIV Corps Report: The Battle for Manila
strengths: Near-contemporaneous official battle report with explicit Japanese defender strength and detailed operational narrative.
limitations: U.S. operational perspective; not a systematic civilian-death or civilian-population reconstruction.
notes: Key denominator anchor for Japanese defenders; also confirms three U.S. divisions converging on the city and that civilians were still present when fire restrictions were initially imposed.

title: Urbanization in Southeast Asia during the World War II period
geographic_scope: Manila city proper within a Southeast Asia comparative study
strengths: Archival demographic reconstruction that collates multiple wartime Manila population estimates and a February 1945 Civil Affairs census.
limitations: Provides stock estimates rather than a day-by-day battle-time civilian presence series; exact timing and coverage of the February 1945 census remain imperfectly specified.
notes: Best available denominator spine for citywide civilian exposure; crucially avoids a Philippines-wide denominator.

title: The 'Death of Manila' in the Second World War and Its Postwar Commemoration
strengths: Brings together battle chronology, war-crimes context, military death totals, civilian death estimate pedigree, and mechanism-of-death discussion.
limitations: The civilian death total is a cited postwar Philippine-government figure rather than an independent micro-level reconstruction.
notes: Most useful source for distinguishing massacre/execution deaths from shelling-related deaths at the narrative level without pretending a clean citywide split exists.

title: Did 100,000 Civilians Die in the Battle of Manila in 1945? Robert Ross Smith's Triumph in the Philippines and the Story of a Number
geographic_scope: Battle of Manila (historiography and source pedigree)
strengths: Best source in this bundle for tracing how the 100,000 figure entered the literature and for surfacing contemporaneous lower estimates and partial burial evidence.
limitations: Primarily a critique of source pedigree, not a full substitute mortality reconstruction; some evidence is relayed through quoted archival or book material rather than reproduced in full.
notes: Essential for uncertainty framing: it shows why the canonical 100,000 figure is influential yet documentarily thin.
